content:
Hi everyone! I just thought you might want to see the progress on the Rust DRM driver I've been working on for the Apple M1 GPU!

It can now run a full GNOME Wayland session, with Firefox, some games like Neverball, KDE apps, and more! There are some cursed hacks involved (hardware related), but with that it runs very stably.

https://twitter.com/LinaAsahi/status/1575345368018997248

:crab::penguin::apple::red_triangle_down:🧊🇼:woman::fire::fox::gear:️ Neverball, Firefox + YouTube, and @Inochi2D, in a GNOME Wayland session!!! All running at the same time, on an Apple M1 Mac Mini with my Rust Linux kernel driver and @alyssarzg's Mesa driver for the M1 GPU!!! :crab::penguin::apple::red_triangle_down:🧊🇼:woman::fire::fox::gear:️ :play:️https://youtu.be/k0cnMUroMlQ https://twitter.com/LinaAsahi/status/1575345368018997248/photo/1

- Asahi Lina / 朝日リナ //nullptr::live (@LinaAsahi)

On the Rust side, I have to say I'm super pleased with my experience writing a driver like this in Rust. I've had zero concurrency issues (and the driver uses fine-grained locking, there's no big driver lock or anything) - once single processes worked, running multiple apps concurrently just worked. Also zero memory leaks (as far as I can tell), dangling CPU or GPU pointers, use-after frees / free order mistakes, or anything like that! The only memory corruption issues I ran into were either fundamental mistakes in my unsafe DRM abstraction or core GPU memory management code, or happened from the GPU side (there's an issue with TLB invalidation, that's what the ugly workaround is for). I feel like Rust encourages good driver design and then the compiler goes a long way towards making the resulting code correct. All in all I didn't really have that many bugs to fix as I wrote the driver, mostly just some logic issues and misunderstandings about how things work on my part (both because I'm new to DRM and because the GPU interface is all reverse engineered and we're still working out the details).

The workaround for the GPU-side TLB inval issue has a large performance hit, but without that, kmscube does run at 1000+ FPS, and that's with a lot of suboptimal components that will be improved over time (e.g. my current allocator allocates/maps/unmaps/frees tons of little GPU structures per frame), so I'm also very optimistic about the performance aspect!

The only major Rust issue I ran into is the lack of placement new, which I ended up working around with a very ugly place!() macro (it still has a soundness issue too, I need to fix it to drop things if initialization fails halfway through). Without that, I was quickly overflowing the kernel stacks (which is particularly ugly to debug without CONFIG_VMAP_STACK, which I didn't have set at first...). With the macro though, the stack frames are under control enough that there's no issue, but I'd really love to see core language support for this. I think it's really necessary for kernel/embedded development.

If anyone is curious, here is the code. The last commit is just a dump of my worktree when things finally worked, and includes some random things that are not necessary (I was blindly trying lots of random changes for the TLB issue), and I'm sure there's a lot of cleanup to be done, but feel free to take a look!

https://github.com/AsahiLinux/linux/tree/gpu/omg-it-works/drivers/gpu/drm/asahi

Your work is amazing!

I think that writing a blog post about your experience would be incredibly valuable. It would show both communities how the current status is and how writing a big driver in Rust is like. It also seems like a lot of work, so if you do not feel like it no problem.

I've been meaning to write one! I just need to find some time for it!

I am giving a talk about this with Alyssa next week at XDC though, so hopefully I can tell at least part of the story there ^^

I am reading through your code and was wondering about the dropping of a GpuObject. IIUC a GpuObject has memory allocated on the GPU (layout of GpuStruct::Raw) and some kernel state (the GpuStruct itself) allocated in a box. When a GpuObject gets dropped, then the kernel state gets dropped just fine, but what about the GpuStruct::Raw? The drop function of GpuObject only logs the dropping and SimpleAllocation also does not drop its contents.

You have to go deeper! SimpleAllocation owns a crate::gem::ObjectRef which itself owns a kernel::drm::gem::ObjectRef which owns a reference to the refcounted shmem::Object GEM object. When the last (only, in the case of GPUStructs) reference to the object is dropped, that first drops the inner DriverObject, which owns the Mapping, which on drop unmaps the pages from the page table, and then calls back into the GEM shmem helper to free the backing pages and the actual storage for the drm::gem::shmem::Object.

You have a point though, that I'm never actually calling drop on GpuStruct::Raw. That's arguably a bug, though I don't see why I'd ever want a Drop impl on those, since they are raw GPU data... (that would belong in the GpuStruct or some parent object). But I guess I should fix that, even though it will probably be a no-op 100% of the time.

Asahi Lina said:

You have to go deeper! SimpleAllocation owns a crate::gem::ObjectRef which itself owns a kernel::drm::gem::ObjectRef which owns a reference to the refcounted shmem::Object GEM object. When the last (only, in the case of GPUStructs) reference to the object is dropped, that first drops the inner DriverObject, which owns the Mapping, which on drop unmaps the pages from the page table, and then calls back into the GEM shmem helper to free the backing pages and the actual storage for the drm::gem::shmem::Object.

Ah that makes sense.
Asahi Lina said:

You have a point though, that I'm never actually calling drop on GpuStruct::Raw. That's arguably a bug, though I don't see why I'd ever want a Drop impl on those, since they are raw GPU data... (that would belong in the GpuStruct or some parent object). But I guess I should fix that, even though it will probably be a no-op 100% of the time.

Was just typing if that was happening. What do you mean by "raw GPU data"? Like just primitives u8 and floats + arrays of those etc? If yes, then you could maybe require a Copy bound on the Raw type.
Copy doesn't work because they contain lifetime-bound GPUPointers and atomics. But yes, the Raw struct is always just primitive types and repr(transparent)/ repr(C) stuff.

You'd never put a Rust object with things like CPU pointers in there, since that would be a security violation (gives the GPU access to CPU pointers), though I'm not sure if there's a way to enforce that in the type system.
you would probably need an unsafe marker trait
But at the moment it is possible to put normal references into the Raw part, right?

Yeah. I guess I should point out that I tried to make the abstractions and general driver design "safe when used as intended", in the sense that they prevent/catch safety issues that could arise by accident, and I think I succeeded at that given how well it runs. But I'm pretty sure there's plenty of holes where you could do something unsafe with crazy / adversarial safe code. At some point there's no real way around that, since in the end you're managing GPU memory and page tables, short of making larger parts of the driver unsafe. But I think that's a net loss, because then you're exposing those parts to safety mistakes within unsafe blocks, or just making everything more verbose with repetitive Safety comments.

Maybe there could be some kind of overarching documentation convention for this? Something like document that GPUStruct::Raw types cannot contain CPU pointers or references, in top-level driver documentation, and just rely on reviewers knowing that? I get the feeling that'd make more sense than marking every single GPUStruct impl unsafe and having the same comment every time.

For example, should the io_pgtable ops be marked unsafe? In a vacuum, they're safe (modulo I'm not sure if you can crash the C backend by doing bad things with them, I know it at least WARNs if you try to unmap something twice, but I don't see a way to make that safer from Rust without a significantly heavier weight abstraction that would put constraints on use cases). But the moment you install the page table base pointer into an IOMMU elsewhere in the driver, they become functions that map arbitrary device addresses to arbitrary physical memory pages. Where do you draw the line?

I haven't written abstractions for this yet, but I'll have to write an abstraction for the Device Tree reserved region stuff. In a vacuum, those are just chunks of memory. But for this GPU, one of those chunks happens to contain the page table base pointers. Should that abstraction be unsafe? That depends on what device you're dealing with...

I think there's no getting around the fact that kernel driver authors need to have their own safety model of the hardware they're dealing with, and try to build their driver to map that as best as possible to safe constructs, but there's no "one right answer" for what APIs should be safe and unsafe when dealing with hardware.

In general I think we should keep the "if you only write safe code then nothing bad can happen" from normal Rust (bad refers to memory errors). Yes that is going to put a lot of noise around the APIs because of safety comments.
But it will overall result in a lot clearer APIs.

I fear that as drivers get bigger and more "implicit unsafety" is added to safe functions we are going to end up with some big spaghetti code mess which you also could have just as easily written in C++.

But that depends on the hardware. Unsafe code can put constraints on otherwise safe code. io_pgtable is safe (ignoring what I said about C side robustness, should be checked) - you can't corrupt memory just by using that API. But if you take the number that API spits out, and put it into a hardware register, suddenly the functions on that object are unsafe. Does that mean your driver should wrap the entire thing in an unsafe API in the same module that installs the pointer? There's no way to enforce that locally. My driver does keep the innards in mmu.rs and only exposes an API that maps pages from a shmem::SGTableIter to the rest of the driver, and that iterator is guaranteed to yield valid memory pages that can be mapped by the hardware. But there's a hole, the map functions return a Mapping object and that must not outlive the shmem::Object that yielded the SGTableIter. That's not that easy to fix, because if I put a reference to the Object in the Mapping that creates a circular reference and breaks freeing objects - right now I rely on those Mappings being stored inside the Object itself (in the inner DriverObject) so they get dropped before the object itself is. I could probably rework the design to avoid this though. But you can see how there's no real way to enforce this kind of safety from the outer abstraction layer. In the end the driver design has to be sound.
Are you talking about the FlushOps trait from the io_pgtable module?

No, just the functions on IOPageTable itself, but those flush_ops are also relevant - they need to make certain guarantees when the page table is used with real hardware, but only sometimes (you could also ignore them and do your own flushing around calls into the API), and all of this only applies when you actually install the page table into hardware anyway.

I guess the idea of my driver is that I build layers of safety - you don't have to worry about any of this allocation stuff if you just use the GpuObject abstraction and the SimpleAllocator that allocates them. And that's most of the driver, and that's what makes it work well and why I had zero actual memory safety bugs so far that weren't just some major problem with the core code.
But you still need to understand the design and follow basic rules like "don't put CPU pointers into GPU-mapped memory", which isn't that hard.

Asahi Lina said:

No, just the functions on IOPageTable itself, but those flush_ops are also relevant - they need to make certain guarantees when the page table is used with real hardware, but only sometimes (you could also ignore them and do your own flushing around calls into the API), and all of this only applies when you actually install the page table into hardware anyway.

ok, I am not really familiar with building drivers, what would the call path be?

  1. create an IOPagetable (how?)
  2. move it to some special location
  3. set the hardware register such that it is used as the page table
  4. now the functions are unsafe?

Asahi Lina said:

I guess the idea of my driver is that I build layers of safety - you don't have to worry about any of this allocation stuff if you just use the GpuObject abstraction and the SimpleAllocator that allocates them. And that's most of the driver, and that's what makes it work well and why I had zero actual memory safety bugs so far that weren't just some major problem with the core code.

that is good design, now if you change the allocation stuff to unsafe, people will naturally move to use the safe abstractions.

The IOPageTable is just a structure in memory, it doesn't do anything on its own. You can mutate it at will with its functions. But it represents a mapping from virtual to physical memory, in principle. Once you point some hardware at that structure in memory, suddenly those functions allow you to map arbitrary physical memory to device memory.

The TLB issue with the GPU that I'm having is actually the GPU caching data from that IOPageTable. The results are nasty: the GPU clobbers random kernel structures and makes some lovely crashes. In principle, that means the code calling into IOPageTable (or the flush ops) is incorrect. But all of this is only possible in the first place because that root pointer was installed into a reserved memory region that is "special".

Asahi Lina said:

The IOPageTable is just a structure in memory, it doesn't do anything on its own. You can mutate it at will with its functions. But it represents a mapping from virtual to physical memory, in principle. Once you point some hardware at that structure in memory, suddenly those functions allow you to map arbitrary physical memory to device memory.

Makes sense, but you could make it safe by doing this:
there are two types, IoPageTable and ActiveIoPageTable. The active table has unsafe functions to modify its contents. The only safe way to point a hardware register at an IoPageTable will at the same time change it to an ActiveIoPageTable.
That doesn't work - anything you did in the past to an IoPageTable is retroactively unsafe once you map it into hardware.
Ah ok, why should the operations on an IoPageTable be safe then?

IoPageTable by itself is safe. If you use it as intended it becomes unsafe, but not because there's anything unsafe about it, just because you are blessing it as a source of safety by taking a number from it and using it to give it special privileges.
but you need unsafe to write the hardware register, right?

Sure, or in this case a reserved memory region. But then it's the driver's responsibility to make sure the rest of the design is safe given that implication. And we're back to where I started: that "safe" code becomes unsafe due to the existence of unsafe code, because the unsafe code imposes invariants on the related safe code. And with drivers, this is more complicated than typical unsafe blocks in Rust. So as driver authors we should strive to still limit this safety impact, and that's what I tried to do, but it's not going to be as pretty or as obvious as regular userspace Rust code.
sure, but I see no problem with the current design of IoPageTable.
Because the abstraction does not leak the unsafety to the user.

Sure, and then we agree that its API makes sense as an abstraction. But within a driver, where do you draw the line? Large drivers like this one are complicated, it's not really practical to say something like "every struct impl is safe and sound at the API boundary" or "every module is safe and sound at the module boundary". There are going to be complex interactions. What we can do is limit them to cases where the chance of bugs due to invariant violations is low, because it's easy to naturally meet those invariants given the driver design.

If you take things to the logical extreme, every single GpuObject is potentially unsafe, because mutating data within could potentially be used to exploit the firmware, and even though it has mitigations against escalation to page table manipulation, they aren't perfect, and you could do that and take over physical memory. Should the GpuObject API be unsafe? I think that doesn't make any sense.

y86 said:

Because the abstraction does not leak the unsafety to the user.

But with the GpuStruct::Raw, a user can write some innocuous looking struct that contains a CPU pointer and create it with only safe code.

If the user had to implement

/// # Safety
/// Types that implement this marker trait are only allowed to contain the following data:
/// - other `RawGpuData` types
/// - primitive numbers like `uX`, `iX` and `f32`/`f64`
///
/// notably absent from this list are CPU pointers.
pub unsafe trait RawGpuData {}

then they would have to think twice about what they were doing and the error would be stopped now, or during code review.

It's just forcing you to add unsafe labels everywhere, and then what are you going to do, just have // Safety: I promise I'm not trying to exploit the firmware, but also this is reverse engineered so I might have gotten it wrong, sorry! comments for every single access? ^^

Yeah, that's a separate issue, but as I just described, there are other holes, like that one fundamental to GpuStruct where every mutation callback is potentially unsafe if taken to the logical extreme.
But then again so is merely managing the lifetimes of those structures wrong!

Asahi Lina said:

If you take things to the logical extreme, every single GpuObject is potentially unsafe, because mutating data within could potentially be used to exploit the firmware, and even though it has mitigations against escalation to page table manipulation, they aren't perfect, and you could do that and take over physical memory. Should the GpuObject API be unsafe? I think that doesn't make any sense.

What do you mean by "mutating data within"? Just in general if I have a u64 and increment it?
Yes. For example, that could overflow a firmware buffer, which could be used to exploit the firmware, and ultimately take over physical memory.
(I don't want to go into further details, but given this Zulip is somewhat private: let's just say I have some experience with how far you can go in this direction.)

Asahi Lina said:

Yes. For example, that could overflow a firmware buffer, which could be used to exploit the firmware, and ultimately take over physical memory.

ok that is interesting.
Drivers are complicated! That's what I'm saying - there's no reasonable way to have absolute safety in kernel Rust the way you have it in userland Rust, once you start talking to hardware.

So the best we can do is use Rust's features to make doing the wrong thing as unlikely as possible. Driver bugs in C code happen because C doesn't stop you from doing anything. It's much easier to use Rust's features to make most mistakes a compile time error, and I think the stability of my driver, and how all the concurrency stuff just worked, is evidence that this approach works!

And I'm sure some of the internal design can be improved to make it safer, but I don't think striving for "perfect safety" should be the goal nor a good idea if taken to the extreme, because it's just impossible to go all the way unless you make the whole driver unsafe, and then you're regressing safety everywhere where Rust is helping.
The thing is unsound Rust code means UB, and that means compiler can do anything to ruin your life ;-)
Unsafe != unsound! I don't think anything I'm doing is unsound (if you find anything, please tell me!)
Or rather, Unsound != UB.

just to make sure, this code could create a firmware overflow?:

struct Struct;
struct Raw {
    num: u64,
}
impl GpuStruct for Struct {
    type Raw<'a> = Raw;
}

let mut obj = alloc.new_object(Struct, |_| Raw { num: 0 })?;
for _ in 0..1000_000_000 {
    obj.with_mut(|raw, _| { raw.num += 1; });
}
Yes, if the firmware says num can only be 0..63.
Or maybe it can only be a number you have previously used in another structure. Or maybe it can only be a number you've previously implicitly allocated via another mechanism.
You can't encode such rules in the type system.
ah because you are writing to some possibly specially handled region of memory?
That's what all of those GpuObjects are!
I thought that they are objects living somewhere on the VRAM
Everything there is shared with the firmware and has to follow very specific rules, many of which we don't even understand because this is all reverse engineered
If you cannot get something per Rust safety requirement, that's unsound to Rust, no?
Yes, they are, and the firmware reads them and interprets them
ah sorry, I forgot that the M1 has shared memory...

Boqun Feng said:

If you cannot get something per Rust safety requirement, that's unsound to Rust, no?

Unsound perhaps, but definitely not UB.
UB is a different category.

To give a silly example: you can have a Drop impl that dereferences a garbage pointer on purpose. That's unsound, but it's only UB if the object actually gets dropped. If you mem::forget it and there is no code path where that doesn't happen, there is no UB anywhere.

Asahi Lina said:

UB is a different category.

yeah, unsound = "safe code can cause UB" (https://rust-lang.github.io/unsafe-code-guidelines/glossary.html#soundness-of-code--of-a-library)
Right. And so, in a way, essentially the entire driver is unsound, by its nature. No way around that.
Lots of complex drivers will be like that, unfortunately. It's a fact of life with hardware.
So it's not about making everything sound, it's about making it as unlikely as possible that you will make a mistake the compiler won't catch that will trigger UB or some other badness.
That's why I'm saying Rust's type system and safety guarantees are less guarantees, and more of a tool we can use in the kernel to significantly reduce the actual incidence of safety-related bugs.
This is different for some parts of core kernel code of course, some things just deal with pure data in memory or structure, and those can be made 100% sound just like userspace Rust is

y86 said:

Asahi Lina said:

UB is a different category.

yeah, unsound = "safe code can cause UB" (https://rust-lang.github.io/unsafe-code-guidelines/glossary.html#soundness-of-code--of-a-library)

But that proves my point: safe code can cause UB if the code is unsound, so unsound means if all you use is safe Rust code, you may still have UB, right?
But when dealing with memory management, hardware, and things like that... the perfect soundness interface ends up being pretty high up.
In the case of this driver, perfect soundness has to exist at the userspace interface.
(For it to be secure)
And so I try to use the type system to help me with that as much as possible.

Boqun Feng said:

y86 said:

Asahi Lina said:

UB is a different category.

yeah, unsound = "safe code can cause UB" (https://rust-lang.github.io/unsafe-code-guidelines/glossary.html#soundness-of-code--of-a-library)

But that proves my point: safe code can cause UB if the code is unsound, so unsound means if all you use is safe Rust code, you may still have UB, right?

if your code is unsound, there is the possibility for UB, it is not guaranteed.

Boqun Feng said:

y86 said:

Asahi Lina said:

UB is a different category.

yeah, unsound = "safe code can cause UB" (https://rust-lang.github.io/unsafe-code-guidelines/glossary.html#soundness-of-code--of-a-library)

But that proves my point: safe code can cause UB if the code is unsound, so unsound means if all you use is safe Rust code, you may still have UB, right?

Yes, but only if the way you use it actually causes that UB. It's not automatically UB.
How do you prove there is no UB then?
If the code is unsound.
By reading the safe code, just like you'd read the unsafe code to prove that there is no UB there.

I can't actually prove that to you, because I can't prove that my understanding of the firmware interface is perfect. So if the bar for Rust code in the kernel is proving it is sound, I'm afraid this driver can never be merged!
@Asahi Lina do you think that most of the unsoundness stems from the fact that this driver is reverse engineered?

Not really, even if I had perfect knowledge of the firmware interface, there would still be a large fraction of code that needs to uphold those guarantees, and by definition all that code would be required to uphold those invariants. It would just be possible to formally prove correctness in theory (in practice, that would probably be a research project on its own for something like GPU firmware, and that's assuming the firmware side is perfect, of course).

Again, this isn't special to drivers! Just look at any random unsafe block in rust/kernel, many of them say something to the effect of "this is safe per such and such invariant", and that invariant is upheld by code not in an unsafe block elsewhere. As long as it doesn't leak the unsoundness across the API, it's fine.
Mostly likely the unsoundness is caused by lack of some abstraction that programmers can use to describe what they want.
Like the sleep context issue we discussed

With drivers it's the same thing - as long as we don't leak the unsoundness to userspace, it's fine. But obviously if we only cared about that, we'd be back to writing C code where any mistake means an exploit, and the entire driver has to be considered in a single safety analysis. So as I said, the goal should be to use Rust type system features to compartmentalize that unsoundness to auditable bits. But you won't be able to keep all those as pretty and self-contained as the unsafe blocks you find in kernel/rust, because drivers like this one are too complicated and some soundness requirements necessarily cross module boundaries, for better or for worse.

Asahi Lina said:

I can't actually prove that to you, because I can't prove that my understanding of the firmware interface is perfect. So if the bar for Rust code in the kernel is proving it is sound, I'm afraid this driver can never be merged!

I'm not trying to make your life harder ;-) It is just that if one has to break Rust safety requirement to get the work, that means something still lacks, and probably push Rust people harder to get it ;-)
There is no way to do this with hardware... It's not something that can be solved by a programming language.

Asahi Lina said:

Not really, even if I had perfect knowledge of the firmware interface, there would still be a large fraction of code that needs to uphold those guarantees, and by definition all that code would be required to uphold those invariants. It would just be possible to formally prove correctness in theory (in practice, that would probably be a research project on its own for something like GPU firmware, and that's assuming the firmware side is perfect, of course).

Let me rephrase my question: Aside from quirky firmware behavior, (and more in general, because I have never written a driver) where does the unsoundness that you expose to the user come from?
Who is the user?
of the driver

Asahi Lina said:

But you won't be able to keep all those as pretty and self-contained as the unsafe blocks you find in kernel/rust, because drivers like this one are too complicated and some soundness requirements necessarily cross module boundaries, for better or for worse.

What other unsoundness is leaking out of the modules?
Thanks for taking the time and answering all of my questions!
As in userspace? Hopefully no unsoundness is leaking there, otherwise that would be a security hole!
But didnt you say that incrementing a GpuObject could lead to firmware register overflow?
When I say module, by the way, I mean Rust module, not kernel module
Yes, but userspace doesn't get to do that!

Asahi Lina said:

When I say module, by the way, I mean Rust module, not kernel module

thats what I understood

Asahi Lina said:

Yes, but userspace doesn't get to do that!

ah ok, what is a graphic application allowed to write then?
So a GpuObject is always something special to control the hardware?

GPU memory is divided into a kernel area and a user area. Most GpuObjects are allocated in the kernel area and only the kernel can write to them, those are the ones shared with firmware and critical for safety. Most user are objects are raw GEM objects owned by userspace, allocated in a specific per-process context, so one GPU process cannot corrupt the kernel or another process (there's a problem with GPU faults breaking other GPU jobs but that's a firmware bug I can't do anything about, we'll shove it under the rug. It's not a memory safety issue, it just means one GPU process can cause another one to fail something). A few objects are allocated by the kernel into a special segment of user area; in principle (if the firmware isn't buggy), nothing the user potentially does with those can cause unsoundness other than a GPU fault for that user.

My driver doesn't even ever read those, only write them, so as long as that holds it can't be affected by user-initiated abuse - but again, I don't know of any reasonable way of encoding this in the type system that would be worth doing. It's literally a few lines of code in one module that write to those buffers, there's no point in going out of our way to make reads impossible if it isn't a trivial change, just for the sake of that.

Maybe the easy version would be to just have a WritableU32 type or something that can only be written, but then you'd still rely on the code actually using that type when allocating userspace buffers. Trying to throw traits around and function variants to make sure if you allocate in a user area you can only use types like that seems like more trouble than it's worth, especially given how rare this is.

Asahi Lina said:

There is no way to do this with hardware... It's not something that can be solved by a programming language.

I don't get it, "soundness" is whether the type system plus everything else in the language think the code is safe or not. If you could have a driver that doesn't have UB, but the language says it's unsound, that's the issue of the language, right?

And so again I go back to my premise: that the goal should be to use Rust's type system to catch all the bugs that are likely to happen, but trying to strive for perfect compiler-enforced safety/soundness everywhere is going to be an endless chase that will never reach its goal.
Soundness is a property of an abstraction, not a property of code!

Asahi Lina said:

Soundness is a property of an abstraction, not a property of code!

But that abstraction will need be applied to code.
There is no such thing as unsound code. There is such a thing as an unsound API/abstraction.
"Accordingly, we say that a library (or an individual function) is sound if it is impossible for safe code to cause Undefined Behavior using its public API. "
Yes. So the public API is a fundamental part of the definition of soundness.
A driver's public API is the userspace API. That has to be sound for the driver to be secure.
But there is no set rule for what internal APIs within the driver need to be sound in a stand-alone way.
So the implementation won't effect the soundness, that's what you are saying?
As long as you have a sound public API

Asahi Lina said:

GPU memory is divided into a kernel area and a user area. Most GpuObjects are allocated in the kernel area and only the kernel can write to them, those are the ones shared with firmware and critical for safety. Most user are objects are raw GEM objects owned by userspace, allocated in a specific per-process context, so one GPU process cannot corrupt the kernel or another process (there's a problem with GPU faults breaking other GPU jobs but that's a firmware bug I can't do anything about, we'll shove it under the rug. It's not a memory safety issue, it just means one GPU process can cause another one to fail something). A few objects are allocated by the kernel into a special segment of user area; in principle (if the firmware isn't buggy), nothing the user potentially does with those can cause unsoundness other than a GPU fault for that user.

that is reassuring! If the users of the driver do not need to handle the GpuObjects. And doing anything with a GpuObject might result in the firmware giving up. Then I think it would be ok to mark GpuObject operations as unsafe with the safety requirement "The caller needs to ensure that this operation is blessed by the firmware". I think it would better capture the nature of the operations you are doing.

After grepping a bit through your repository, as you described these are all over the place. I am not sure if this can be done, but I think it could be possible to create newtypes for GpuObject<MyStruct, _> and implement safe operations on those.

It will, which is why in typical self-contained Rust modules we have all those safety comments that spell out rules that the rest of the code (within the same implementation) has to follow, which a human can check, to gauge soundness.

What I'm saying is this is a lot messier when hardware is involved. You can't break it down into little simple, auditable, fully self-contained soundness checks. In the end, soundness relies on complex relationships that span the whole driver, and there's no way around that. But what we can do is make the design easier to understand, and use the type system to catch as many as possible of those mistakes that would make the driver unsound. Which is a much, much better state of affairs than C, and that's why Rust is awesome for drivers!

y86 said:

that is reassuring! If the users of the driver do not need to handle the GpuObjects. And doing anything with a GpuObject might result in the firmware giving up. Then I think it would be ok to mark GpuObject operations as unsafe with the safety requirement "The caller needs to ensure that this operation is blessed by the firmware". I think it would better capture the nature of the operations you are doing.

But what would that buy me? It would just mean every single access of a GpuObject has to be marked unsafe. Then what, add a comment explaining why everything I'm doing is right? I can't even do that with this firmware I'm reverse engineering! The best I can do is hope my current understanding is correct. And it's not just the actual GpuObject operations, it's the relationship between them as well. For example, sometimes dropping certain GpuObjects has to be accompanied by sending the firmware a message to stop using them first. That means the act of dropping would have to be unsafe, which is not something Rust can even represent!

That's why I'm saying none of this is perfect, we still need humans to audit and understand driver design, but Rust makes it infinitely easier to compartmentalize these things in a way that we can at least plausibly audit them and be much more confident about correctness than with a C driver ^^

Asahi Lina said:

That's why I'm saying none of this is perfect, we still need humans to audit and understand driver design, but Rust makes it infinitely easier to compartmentalize these things in a way that we can at least plausibly audit them and be much more confident about correctness than with a C driver ^^

We all agree on that! ;-)

The fact that all this, in practice, isn't crashing left and right due to race conditions and corner cases, is amazing. I'm no genius programmer, if I'd written this in C it would've been extremely broken on the first pass, I guarantee. But just going from one GPU app to two, and seeing alloc and render ops interleaved in dmesg, and nothing crashing or even deadlocking or anything, is proof that Rust is the future for the kernel. Not because it magically made it correct for me (I was still careful with the driver design so it could handle multiprocessing correctly!), but because it stopped me from making mistakes when implementing it!
And we probably eventually choose what you said, simply admit that Rust safety cannot cover all, it's helpful but kernel Rust code still needs humans to audit.

Asahi Lina said:

y86 said:
And it's not just the actual GpuObject operations, it's the relationship between them as well. For example, sometimes dropping certain GpuObjects has to be accompanied by sending the firmware a message to stop using them first. That means the act of dropping would have to be unsafe, which is not something Rust can even represent!

If the creation is unsafe then one of the safety guarantees can be "do not drop me wrongly".

Sure, but again, if every GpuObject creation is unsafe then... what does that buy us? It's just a token in the source code... in the end it still only applies to certain cases, and you have to understand that given the firmware API.

And on top of that, unsafe scope leakage is a problem at this level. You end up making the code a lot more awkward if you want to limit the unsafe block only to the code that is actually unsafe. Just look at how some of those GpuObjects are created... Some have hundreds of fields...
yes, but code is read far more often than written. It is a lot easier for reviewers to see "ok here we have firmware stuff, let me first understand that, before I audit this part".

Yeah, but that applies to every GpuObject... In my opinion that's something for a top-level design doc to desribe in prose, not for unsafe tags and little comments littered everywhere. Anyone looking at this driver should understand that every GpuObject represents a firmware struct (almost all the time) and implicit rules have to be followed.

Because as I said, even if you make GpuObject unsafe, then you're not going to be able to make every user of those objects' API perfectly safe. It gets messy at the top level, and it makes sense and is reasonably auditable if you understand how the firmware works, but just littering lots of APIs with unsafe there doesn't actually improve things.

Asahi Lina said:

And on top of that, unsafe scope leakage is a problem at this level. You end up making the code a lot more awkward if you want to limit the unsafe block only to the code that is actually unsafe. Just look at how some of those GpuObjects are created... Some have hundreds of fields...

Agreed. I understand unsafe might be the best we can have, but sometimes you'd prefer not having it ;-) The usage of it is just too arbitrary
"You have two APIs that cannot be used back-to-back, which one should you mark as unsafe?"
Yup!

So in the end my approach here is, okay, unsafe is for things that directly lead to kernel-side memory safety violations if misused, or other immediate badness. And then, on top of that, to encourage sound driver design, I use the type system and other Rust features as much as possible to make handling GpuObjects correctly easy (that's why they have that Raw struct and an attached kernel struct, because the kernel side is what has ownership over pointed to data and the GpuPointer enforces that via lifetimes. GpuWeakPointer does not, because sometimes it's not practical to go with compiler-enforced lifetimes everywhere, and then you rely on the user managing object lifetime correctly). And it doesn't make sense to use unsafe for those things. In a way, inside this driver, GpuWeakPointer means unsafe, but a very specific class of unsafe, which the author/reviewer has to understand. And maybe there should be comments for that too (there should probably be lots of comments in this driver anyway, I've just been in crazy dev mode and didn't have time to do proper documentation yet, but I absolutely should so others can make sense of this...), but I don't think upgrading it to unsafe makes sense here. Finer-grained safety annotations are a good thing, and there's no expectation that someone unfamiliar with this GPU could pick up the driver and start hacking on firmware structures without first learning about it anyway! That's where some prose documentation helps, I think ^^

Most of the GpuWeakPointer usages are easy to audit. It's either backpointers to an owning object anyway, or stuff like the global GPU channels (which are initialized once and never go away, it was just too annoying to work that into the lifetime-bound GpuPointer and doesn't buy you anything), or side-pointers further down the chain of ownership to something you already own a reference to anyway.

But this whole thing, with GpuPointer as the default type for firmware structure references, and the design (which a bunch of really knowledgeable Rust folks helped me out with in a stream way back, where we built a userspace prototype - I'm a Rust newbie, I couldn't have come up with this lifetime idea/implementation on my own), makes you think of firmware structures in terms of owning other structures or Refs to them, and that drives the whole design in a direction where even if you have to use weak pointers sometimes that don't enforce the rules, in the end the rules are followed and it's easy to see that. And I really, really like that about Rust. That I can now look at the driver and think "yeah, this matches my understanding of firmware structure lifetimes, and I don't care what the rest of the driver is doing, I know this pointer isn't going to become invalid until that gets dropped and that means this was already dropped, QED".

Where "I don't care what the rest of the driver is doing" is qualified by "as long as it isn't doing something completely ridiculous like tracing through pointer chains in violation of basic hygiene and swapping pointers out from under me", because why on earth would it be doing that? I can assume the driver isn't being deliberately malicious with my abstractions/concepts. The goal is to make sure that doesn't happen by mistake ^^

For the time being I think it is ok to write drivers like that.

In the long term, I will need to think about if there is anything that can be done to improve the situation with littering unsafe everywhere and still marking firmware interactions as unsafe.
(random thought: what if we would allow "implicit unsafe", so you declare a function as unsafe(firmware_interaction) and then at the call site module you are allowed to write #![allow_implicit_unsafe(firmware_interaction)]. then you would only add one line to each of the modules (or functions if you want to be more granular) and it would compile without complaining about the function being unsafe. If someone else writes in that module, they would of course need to be aware of that attribute, but grepping for unsafe would still mention this usage and reviewers would also be able to see usages with the help of some tools (e.g. a rustc option warn_on_implicit_unsafe_usage). So in essence it would still be marked as unsafe, but without the need to always write safety comments etc.)
That could be useful, though... that ends up being a lot of the driver, so I'm not sure that you wouldn't just end up with every function marked like that, in effect.
Or at least every function that matters and has actual complexity needing humans to review it... which somewhat defeats the purpose ^^
I think we also need to consider future drivers, they could have different architectures where they also need to compute something else without interacting with the unsafe bits at all.

Of course, but then you could basically just declare that firmware interaction at the top of the module. I mean, if you just want to be able to grep for unsafe, just have a local rule that modules with those interactions need to have that tag at the top in a comment.
And then if a module is supposed to have a sound API internally, you can state that intention.

Personally, if we want to look for Rust language features to help with kernel programming... I think that stuff about context objects would be #1 on my list, for sleepable/atomic/etc tracking. That would help eliminate deadlocks and scheduling mode violations, which is a major issue in kernel code (I've found bugs like that in existing C code just running with lockdep on...) and that absolutely needs language support to work, due to the Drop corner case I mentioned which can't be worked around with macros as far as I can tell. Actually, as a related feature, I'd love to be able to mark objects as outright illegal to drop - then the compiler would error out if you ever drop them, and you'd have to consume them via a dedicated function instead. That would allow for the context problem to be solved, at the cost of making code a lot more verbose by explicitly marking all those drop points, since you could pass the context around via a regular argument at that point. Of course, that also means that any parent objects would also be required to manually implement Drop if they contain any such undroppable object. And then I could mark my GpuContextData struct specifically as undroppable, and force callers to go through a function that consumes it and pings the firmware about it first.
If we're talking Rust features to help with this kind of development, those are the places I'd go first, not trying to do finer-grained tracking of relative safety ^^
yes you are right, there are bigger issues at hand. But it just does not sit right with me to not declare something as unsafe when it is unsafe.
When doing that would end up with most of the driver as unsafe... what is unsafe even? ^^
For better or for worse, safety is a lot more relative in the kernel than it is in userspace.

And this isn't new to Rust - it's completely normal to have safe code alongside unsafe code, where the safe code turns everything unsound if it is wrong. That's fundamental to how safety works in Rust.
So in the end it's just about internal API boundaries, and which ones should be sound and which ones shouldn't. And, well, that's a driver development/design decision.
(Just as it is, at the end of the day, the same decision for any other Rust crate/module)
Drivers just tend to be messier about this, but they aren't fundamentally different at the end of the day!

Actually, as a related feature, I'd love to be able to mark objects as outright illegal to drop

If you are fine with a linker error, then you can use

struct NoDrop;

impl Drop for NoDrop {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        extern "C" {
            fn trigger() -> !;
        }
        unsafe { trigger() }
    }
}
Does that actually work?
Huh.
reliably only in release though
I might try that as an experiment.
the linker errors are horrible, so lang support would be way better
Yeah, of course.
In debug I would just panic!() instead, but that was in userspace
But for firmware structs I have this one case where I'd use this, so it's worth the experiment.
Wouldn't the problem in debug be the opposite, that you get a linker error even if it never gets called, since it didn't get optimized out?
I don't think you'd ever actually get it to link when it shouldn't
yes it would be a false positive
Oh, you mean panic instead, right.
It could be a big kernel warn in debug mode if it comes to that.
But I think as long as the linker is removing unused functions, it should always link?
Worth a shot
depends on the exact use case. why is this struct not allowed to be dropped?
Because it's "unsafe" to drop without notifying the firmware
ah right
but mem::forgetting would be fine?
So instead I'd force the drop point to do foo.release(some_device_context) (details TBD) such that it can send the message before dropping itself.
Yeah, that would actually be fine, it'd just leak
The firmware pointer wouldn't become invalid
ok then this approach would work (you need to remember that the function consuming the object needs to mem::forget(self) at the end)

Yup, exactly. I'd have to work out the details of how all that interacts with GpuObject, I can't say right now that the traits would work out (I've run into problems with the lack of negative traits before), but I won't know until I try it.
Since of course I don't actually want to forget it, I want the backing memory to be dropped and all the other machinery to run, I just want to make it inaccessible to the user.
You could also add a NoDrop guard as a field and in the consuming function only forget the guard.

Yeah, but then I still need to prevent Drop from being auto-derived for everything up to the GpuObject I think? Unless all that gets linked out if never used, which I guess it would be... but then I still need a way to make it work for something taking the actual GpuObject... Anyway, there are details to work out here. I won't know until I try it.

It wouldn't work with a NoDrop guard inside an Option<> because the call to the Drop would still exist, just in a codepath that never actually gets called, and I don't think that'd be reliably optimized out.
So I think it needs to just pollute the struct directly and then cleanup might be tricky
We'll see!

No problem! I think I'm kind of weird in writing such a big driver in Rust and there's probably not a lot of people with experience with both driver and Rust camps, so I'm happy to share what I've learned. I should really write a blog post about this...

y86 said:

Where is the review of this driver going to happen (is it already ongoing?)? Are you simply going to create a PR on github?

It will be a long time before the driver is ready for proper mainlining, since we need to design a stable uAPI with Vulkan support for it and prove it works (since the uAPI is subject to the kernel userspace interface stability guarantees, getting it wrong is a huge pain). Once it gets to that point I imagine we'll do the review on the mailing lists, the normal kernel way, with all the DRM folks involved too.

But in the meantime, I welcome ad-hoc code reviews! I would love to get some opinions on how I do things. Maybe people here can just comment directly on GitHub? Or maybe once it's about ready for our end-users to start testing (ETA: a couple months) I can create an internal PR in the Asahi Linux repo for marcan to merge it into the official branch, and anyone is welcome to review the code at that point?
